title: Conversion (Wrongful Interference with Goods)

statuteRef.label: Time limit for actions in tort — conversion and extinction of title

questions.label: Date of the conversion
questions.helpText: The date on which the defendant wrongfully interfered with, took possession of, or destroyed the claimant's goods. If the goods were converted by a series of dealings, each dealing may give rise to a fresh cause of action.

questions.label: Was the claimant under a disability at the date of accrual?
questions.helpText: Under the Limitation Act 1980, a person is under a disability if they are a minor (under 18) or lack capacity within the meaning of the Mental Capacity Act 2005.

questions.label: Date disability ceased
questions.helpText: The date the claimant turned 18, or the date capacity was regained.

questions.label: Was there fraud, deliberate concealment, or mistake?
questions.helpText: Under s.32 Limitation Act 1980, if the defendant deliberately concealed the conversion or the claimant's right to the goods, the limitation period does not begin until the claimant discovered or could with reasonable diligence have discovered the concealment.

questions.label: Date of discovery
questions.helpText: The date the claimant discovered, or could with reasonable diligence have discovered, the fraud, concealment, or mistake.

notes: Under s.3 Limitation Act 1980, the original owner's title to goods is extinguished after 6 years from the first conversion.
Each subsequent dealing with converted goods constitutes a fresh conversion but does not revive the original owner's extinguished title.
Where goods are stolen, the title of the thief (and anyone claiming through them, other than a purchaser in good faith) is never perfected by limitation.
